model_judgments.reasoning: This was a close technical debate. NEG made sophisticated moves, particularly reframing the India-Pakistan case to argue that 'deterrence preventing escalation' proves underlying instability rather than stability. However, this argument relies on NEG winning the definitional dispute about what 'stability' means - a dispute that was never conclusively resolved. AFF's framework (stability = war prevention) is at least equally defensible as NEG's (stability = crisis prevention). AFF's core evidence (Suzuki 2015) showing fewer disputes with more nuclear states was attacked on methodology but never defeated with counter-evidence. NEG failed to maintain all their arguments through the final speeches and dropped several of AFF's original claims. AFF demonstrated better crystallization in the 2AR and more consistent case management throughout. While NEG had stronger individual technical moments, particularly in NC and NR, AFF's overall debate performance was slightly more complete.

key_moments.description: Definition of stability remained contested but AFF's framing (wars prevented = stability) was more intuitive and accessible
AFF's Suzuki evidence was attacked but never defeated - NEG critiqued methodology without providing counter-data
NEG's India-Pakistan reframing was sophisticated but relied on winning an unresolved definitional dispute
NEG dropped AFF's preventive war argument and decreasing aggression claims
AFF showed superior crystallization and maintained case consistency throughout

model_judgments.reasoning: The Affirmative won the debate by securing the core conceptual clash over the definition of 'stability' and leveraging the Negative's strategic errors. When the Negative introduced the India-Pakistan dyad to prove instability, the Affirmative effectively turned the argument by pointing out that the de-escalation of those crises proved deterrence prevents major wars. The Negative's case was further undermined by citing evidence that contradicted their own claims (the Pakistan government quote) and misapplying evidence (the Johnson 2022 AI card). Finally, the Negative's decision to drop their NPT and arms control arguments in the NR left them with no independent offense to outweigh the Affirmative's empirical claims.

model_judgments.reasoning: I vote NEG because the debate is ultimately about how to evaluate “international stability.” NEG successfully reframes stability as low crisis frequency/low brinkmanship and argues proliferation shifts conflict into repeated high-risk crises that deterrence only contains. AFF responds that avoiding major war is the right metric and uses de-escalation episodes as proof deterrence stabilizes, but AFF does not sufficiently win the standard comparison—why crisis containment should outweigh crisis prevention as the controlling interpretation of stability. That framing loss matters because AFF’s main empirical claim (Suzuki 2015: more nuclear states correlate with fewer disputes) is then vulnerable to NEG’s operationalization critique (counting major wars/disputes while missing frequent crises). AFF never clearly resolves that methodological dispute with in-round specificity, so I discount the force of “fewer disputes = more stability.” On the India–Pakistan evidence, both sides can spin de-escalation, but under NEG’s metric the repeated crises are affirmative proof of instability. AFF’s late Cohen-based takeout is not developed enough in the shared record to outweigh the framing and application advantages NEG maintains. Therefore NEG wins on the most important evaluative question and its consistent offense under that standard.

key_moments.description: NEG wins the more decisive stability metric (crisis prevention vs mere war avoidance), and AFF does not adequately justify why its metric should control
NEG’s operationalization critique of AFF’s key empirical claim (Suzuki: disputes/major war vs crises) is not cleanly answered with specific in-round defense, mitigating AFF’s primary advantage
India–Pakistan examples are at least equally consistent with NEG’s “instability narrowly averted” interpretation under NEG’s metric

voting_issues: NEG wins the more decisive stability metric (crisis prevention vs mere war avoidance), and AFF does not adequately justify why its metric should control
NEG’s operationalization critique of AFF’s key empirical claim (Suzuki: disputes/major war vs crises) is not cleanly answered with specific in-round defense, mitigating AFF’s primary advantage
India–Pakistan examples are at least equally consistent with NEG’s “instability narrowly averted” interpretation under NEG’s metric
